coredata.dc:title: Bright yellow fluorescent nitrogen and boron doped-CNPs based turn “off–on” probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of glyphosate and hypochlorite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Nandhini, Karuppasamy
Ilanchelian, Malaichamy
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, water-soluble fluorescent nitrogen/boron-doped carbon nano particles (N/B-CNPs) were prepared using o-phenylenediamine and boric acid precursors by a simple one step hydrothermal (HT) method. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) analysis showed that the N/B-CNPs are spherical in shape with an average size of ∼ 15 nm. The functional and structural composition of N/B-CNPs were confirmed by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), Raman and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectral studies. N/B-CNPs exhibited excellent photophysical property and photostability at different physiological conditions. N/B-CNPs showed bright yellow fluorescence at 566 nm upon excitation at 400 nm. N/B-CNPs were utilized as a fluorometric probe towards the simultaneous detection of glyphosate and hypochlorite. The fluorescence intensity of N/B-CNPs was selectively quenched in the presence of glyphosate (turn-off) and quenched emission intensity was restored in the presence of hypochlorite (turn-on). The fluorescence quenching mechanism was further ascertained using Stern-Volmer and time resolved fluorescence analysis. The detection probe showed a linear response for glyphosate over the concentrations range of 5.00 to 85.00 µM, with a limit of detection value of 0.088 µM. While in the case of hypochlorite the turn-on assay exhibited good linearity over the range of 5.00 to 50.00 µM with limit of detection as low as 0.12 nM. The N/B-CNPs turn-off/turn-on fluorometric assay exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards glyphosate and hypochlorite, even in the presence of other interference ions. The practical applicability of the proposed assay was demonstrated by fabricating a paper strip-based sensor and fluorescent thin film. Furthermore, the in vivo toxicity of the N/B-CNPs was evaluated in plant by performing dosage dependent assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N/B-CNPs
Glyphosate
Hypochlorite
Turn off–on sensor
Thin film

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of quantum yield of N/B-CNPs
Morphology and functional groups analysis of N/B-CNPs
N/B-CNPs As fluorescent probe for glyphosate (turn-off)
Selectivity of the N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system probe (turn-on)
Fluorescent N/B-CNPs paper strip and polymer thin film
In vivo toxicity and bio-imaging of N/B-CNPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: K. Nandhini acknowledges the project RUSA-2.0, BCTRC Allotment number-CT26A, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India for financial support. Prof. M. Ilanchelian acknowledges the DST-SERB-EEQ project no. EEQ/2021/000469, dt: 02.03.2022 India for the financial support. We gratefully thank and dedicate it to our beloved Dr. S. Abraham John, Professor, Gandhigram Rural Institute, Dindigul, India for his kind support, encouragement and valuable guidance. Authors acknowledges the NMR facility, CIC, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, supported by DST-PURSE Phase-II program. Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology Madras is acknowledged for providing lifetime analysis.

K. Nandhini acknowledges the project RUSA-2.0, BCTRC Allotment number-CT26A, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India for financial support. Prof. M. Ilanchelian acknowledges the DST-SERB-EEQ project no. EEQ/2021/000469, dt: 02.03.2022 India for the financial support. We gratefully thank and dedicate it to our beloved Dr. S. Abraham John, Professor, Gandhigram Rural Institute, Dindigul, India for his kind support, encouragement and valuable guidance. Authors acknowledges the NMR facility, CIC, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, supported by DST-PURSE Phase-II program. Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology Madras is acknowledged for providing lifetime analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (i) UV-visible absorption spectrum of N/B-CNPs and (ii) Emission and excitation spectra of N/B-CNPs (Inset: photographic image of N/B-CNPs under (a) visible light and (b) UV light 365 nm).
(i) HR-TEM image, (ii) Particle size histogram of N/B-CNPs and (iii) HR-TEM micrograph of N/B-CNPs.
(i) FT-IR image of N/B-CNPs and (ii) Raman spectrum of N/B-CNPs.
(i) 1H NMR and (ii) 13C NMR spectra of N/B-CNPs.

(i) Emission spectral measurements of N/B-CNPs with different concentrations of glyphosate. [Glyphosate] (a) 0.00, (b) 5.00, (c) 10.00, (d) 15.00, (e) 20.00, (f) 25.00, (g) 30.00, (h) 35.00, (i) 40.00, (j) 45.00, (k) 50.00, (l) 55.00, (m) 60.00, (n) 65.00, (o) 70.00, (p) 75.00, (q) 80.00 and (r) 85.00 µM (ii) Linear plot of ΔF vs concentrations of glyphosate the concentrations range from 5.00 to 85.00 µM.
Time resolved fluorescence decay curves of N/B-CNPs before and after addition of glyphosate/ClO-.
The influence of various interferences on the emission intensity of the N/B-CNPs/Gp system. The error bars represented the relative standard deviation of three experiments that were repeated.

(i) Emission spectra of N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system under different concentrations of ClO-. [ClO-] (a) 0.00, (b) 5.00, (c) 10.00, (d) 15.00, (e) 20.00, (f) 25.00, (g) 30.00, (h) 35.00, (i) 40.00, (j) 45.00 and (k) 50.00 µM and (ii) Linear calibration plot of N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system with ClO- 5.00 to 50.00 µM.
N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system, in the presence of other anion interferences.
(i) N/B-CNPs fluorescent polymer thin film, (ii) Fluorescence N/B-CNPs based test paper strips turn-off (glyphosate) and turn-on (ClO-).

(i) Effects of N/B-CNPs on the mung bean seeds growth after 5 days (Different concentrations) (ii) mung bean length of root (iii) mung bean length of stem and (iv & v) Fluorescent N/B-CNPs in mung bean seed live imaging in plant systems compared with control group.
Schematic illustration of N/B-CNPs as a probe for the detection of glyphosate (gp) and hypochlorite (ClO-)
Possible formation mechanism of N/B-CNPs from o-phenylenediamine and boric acid precursor.
N/B-CNPs as a probe detection of glyphosate (Gp) in real water samples.
N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system as a probe detection of ClO- in real water samples

head.ce:title: Bright yellow fluorescent nitrogen and boron doped-CNPs based turn “off–on” probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of glyphosate and hypochlorite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore-641 046, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore-641 046, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, water-soluble fluorescent nitrogen/boron-doped carbon nano particles (N/B-CNPs) were prepared using o-phenylenediamine and boric acid precursors by a simple one step hydrothermal (HT) method. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) analysis showed that the N/B-CNPs are spherical in shape with an average size of ∼ 15 nm. The functional and structural composition of N/B-CNPs were confirmed by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), Raman and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectral studies. N/B-CNPs exhibited excellent photophysical property and photostability at different physiological conditions. N/B-CNPs showed bright yellow fluorescence at 566 nm upon excitation at 400 nm. N/B-CNPs were utilized as a fluorometric probe towards the simultaneous detection of glyphosate and hypochlorite. The fluorescence intensity of N/B-CNPs was selectively quenched in the presence of glyphosate (turn-off) and quenched emission intensity was restored in the presence of hypochlorite (turn-on). The fluorescence quenching mechanism was further ascertained using Stern-Volmer and time resolved fluorescence analysis. The detection probe showed a linear response for glyphosate over the concentrations range of 5.00 to 85.00 µM, with a limit of detection value of 0.088 µM. While in the case of hypochlorite the turn-on assay exhibited good linearity over the range of 5.00 to 50.00 µM with limit of detection as low as 0.12 nM. The N/B-CNPs turn-off/turn-on fluorometric assay exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards glyphosate and hypochlorite, even in the presence of other interference ions. The practical applicability of the proposed assay was demonstrated by fabricating a paper strip-based sensor and fluorescent thin film. Furthermore, the in vivo toxicity of the N/B-CNPs was evaluated in plant by performing dosage dependent assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades nanomaterials-based research has exploded as a result of its wide spared applications in the field of chemistry, medicine, optical and biosensor [1]. Carbon atom is found in all kinds of biological life and ever since the inception of nanotechnology carbon has played a significant role in the production of nanomaterials ranging from three-dimensional carbon to one-dimensional nanomaterials [2]. In recent years, the versatile nature of carbon-based nanomaterials has encouraged scientists to investigate their application in different fields including imaging, energy storage, sensing and LEDs [3,4]. Among the various classes of carbon nanomaterials, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) including carbon dots, carbon nano dots and carbon quantum dots has lately gained popularity in a variety of applications [5]. As compared to the traditional carbon-based nanomaterials, fluorescent CNPs possess superior optical properties, good conductivity, high chemical stability, excitation-dependent multicolor emission, good cell permeability, low toxicity and eco-friendly. Moreover, CNPs are much easier to synthesize, functionalize and dope with heteroatoms to achieve multi-color emission [6–9]. For example, CNPs have been prepared from a variety of resources, including synthetic polymers and biomaterials derived from natural resources by simple high temperature pyrolysis process [5,10]. Although, CNPs exhibit attractive properties, which gives the room for improvement still persists. For instance, contrary to the inorganic semiconducting quantum dots, the molecular structures of CNPs are still not properly characterized due to difficulty in predicting the condensation reaction of various carbon sources [11,12]. The synthesis of CDs from phenylenediamine precursors (o, p and meta) have been widely reported with their applications ranging from metal ion detection to cell imaging applications [13,14,22]. However, the use of N/B-CNPs derived from the hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and boric acid in the sequential fluorometric detection of analytes has not yet been reported. Moreover, the present work brings out new information pertaining to the formation mechanism of N/B-CNPs. Further, the QY of N/B-CNPs was found to be much higher than that of the previously reported CQDs generated from the microwave treatment of o-phenylenediamine and boric acid [22]. Furthermore, it has been reported that the doping of atoms such as nitrogen and boron into CNPs can significantly modify their electronic structures and can increase the fluorescence quantum yields [15].

Glyphosate (N-(phosphonomethyl) glycine) is an herbicide developed by Monsanto in 1970 [16]. Glyphosate was used worldwide to suppress weed growth due to its outstanding weeding efficiency and comparatively low toxicity to mammals. Nevertheless, long-term residues from the overuse of glyphosate in agricultural land, on the other hand, pollute the environment and pose health risks [17]. The effect of glyphosate has also been shown in studies to have the ability to change the human endocrine system. Furthermore, in 2015 International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) has listed glyphosate as a possible carcinogenic to humans [18,19]. Apart from herbicides, the chemical disinfectants are most commonly used for the treatment of water. Among this, sodium hypochlorite being an effective antibacterial agent is widely utilized as a disinfectant in water treatment process to eliminate viruses, microorganisms, germs and chemicals in water [20]. However, abnormal level of hypochlorite can lead to various disorders like Alzheimer’s, cardiovascular disease, cancer and arthritis [21]. Therefore, monitoring the trace levels of widely used agricultural herbicides and disinfectants in environmental water bodies assumes greater importance.

Herein, a HT method was utilized for the synthesis of N/B-CNPs. The synthesized material was characterized by various spectroscopy techniques. Highly fluorescent N/B-CNPs are used as a sensitive detection probe for simultaneous detection of glyphosate (turn-off) and hypochlorite (turn-on). The fluorescence quenching mechanism of B/N-CNPs-glyphosate was further studied using spectrofluorometric and time resolved fluorescence studies. Further, phytotoxicity assay and fluorescence imaging studies were carried out to analyze the biocompatibility of N/B-CNPs in model plant mung bean (Vigna Radiata) (Scheme 1 ).

The N/B-CNPs were synthesized using a previously described process with some modifications [22]. Briefly, 16 mg o-phenylenediamine and 10 mg of boric acid were added to 20 mL of double distilled water and then the solution was mixed with vigorous stirring for 10 min at room temperature (25 °C). The resulting solution was then transferred into a stainless-steel Teflon autoclave with a capacity of 50 mL. The autoclave was kept in a hot air oven at 200 °C for 4 h. The autoclave was then allowed to cool naturally to get room temperature and the final reaction solution was washed with dichloromethane to remove unreacted organic precursors. Subsequently, the resultant solution is then subjected to centrifugation for 45 min at 7000 rpm and filtered using Millipore membrane (0.22 µm) to remove larger particles. The aqueous dispersion of N/B-CNPs were stored in the refrigerator for further analysis. The obtained aqueous solution of N/B-CNPs were subjected to freeze drying process and the resultant N/B-CNPs were dissolved in DMSO‑d 6 to record 1H, 13C and 11B - NMR spectra.

A JASCO FP-6600 spectrofluorometer with a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette was used to record the emission spectra of N/B-CNPs. The excitation wavelength was fixed at 400 nm using a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. The emission intensity profile was monitored between 410 and 800 nm with a scanning speed of 500 nm min−1. Throughout the studies, the emission and excitation slit widths were maintained as 5 and 5 nm, respectively. JASCO V-630 UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to acquire absorption spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using quartz cuvettes with a path length of 1.0 cm. The fluorocube - life time system was performed using Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique using a pulsed-diode excitation source of 390 nm (JOBIN-VYON M/S). The morphology of N/B-CNPs were investigated using HR-TEM on a JEOL JEM 2100 microscope with a 200 kV operating voltage. The average particle size was computed using Image J software. The fluorescence images were taken by Fluorescence microscopy (BX51, Olympus, Japan). Surface charge analysis were also done using Horiba scientific instrument, SZ −100 version 2.40. Bruker Avance 400 MHz equipment was used to record the NMR spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of quantum yield of N/B-CNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of N/B-CNPs was measured and calculated by using the fluorescence method using rhodamine 6G as a standard fluorophore (ɸ = 0.95 %). The quantum yields of N/B-CNPs and fluorescein were calculated from the following equation - (1) [22]:
(1)
ɸ s
=
ɸ r
×
A s
A r
f r
f s
ɳ r
2
ɳ r
2
Where, ɸs and ɸr are the fluorescence quantum yield of sample and reference respectively. As and Ar represent the fluorescence curve area of sample and reference emission spectrum respectively. fs and fr denotes the absorption optical density (OD) value for sample and reference. ɳ is the refractive index of the water.

The fluorometric detections were carried out by adding different concentrations of glyphosate into 2.5 mL of double distilled water containing 100 µL of N/B-CNPs. After, 10 min of incubation the solution was subjected to emission spectral analysis. The selective and sensitive detection of N/B-CNPs as a probe towards glyphosate and hypochlorite were evaluated in the presence of common interfering metal cations/anions including Ag+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pd2+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Mg2+,CN–, I-, S2-, Cl-, ClO- and other molecules such as histidine, methionine, threonine, dopamine, fenthion, lysine, phenylalanine, proline, daunorubicin and parathion-methyl. The detection assays (glyphosate and hypochlorite) were performed in triplicate.

The feasibility of N/B-CNPs probe towards the detection of glyphosate in practical applications were tested using water samples collected from Coimbatore (Ground water), Tamilnadu, India. To estimate the concentration of glyphosate in ground water sample, a known quantity of glyphosate was spiked into ground water sample and the recovery of glyphosate was calculated using the standard addition method. Drinking and tap water samples were collected from department of chemistry, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore. Swimming pool water was collected from Harishree Garden residential area, Coimbatore. All these water samples were spiked with a known concentration of hypochlorite. The rate recovery was calculated using the Equation − 2 (Eq. (2)) [23].
(2)
Recovery % =
Conc . o f a n a l y t e f o u n d
Conc . o f a n a l y t e a d d e d
× 100

The photophysical characteristics of N/B-CNPs were evaluated using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic analysis. Fig. 1 (i) shows the UV – vis absorption spectrum of N/B-CNPs, which exhibits two distinct absorption bands around at 233 and 286 nm, which are ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic group present in the N/B-CNPs [24–26]. In addition, the weak absorption band located at 420 nm is ascribed to the aromatic ring structure namely N-H, B-OH, respectively [22]. It can be seen from Fig. 1.(ii), the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of N/B-CNPs are obtained at 400 and 566 nm, respectively. The synthesized N/B-CNPs appeared clear in visible light when irradiated under UV lamp, it showed a bright yellow fluorescence. The fluorescence analysis revealed that both the emission maxima and fluorescence emission intensity of N/B-CNPs varied with respect to the change in the excitation wavelength from 380 − 490 nm (Fig. S1). It can be seen that upon excitation at 400 nm, the N/B-CNPs showed a strongest fluorescence emission intensity at 566 nm. As shown in Fig. S1 the emission maxima of N/B-CNPs remained unchanged and only the change in emission intensity was observed, indicating the narrow size distribution of the particles. Subsequently, so as to get a maximum signal amplification from N/B-CNPs the excitation wavelength was fixed at 400 nm.

The photostability of N/B-CNPs were evaluated under different physiological conditions including varying salt concentrations, different irradiation time, pH and incubation time and are displayed in Fig. S2. The fluorescence intensity of N/B-CNPs remained stable upon an increasing range of ionic strengths (Fig. S2(i)). Furthermore, the fluorescence emission intensity of N/B-CNPs exhibits significant stability even after 60 min of continuous irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. S2(ii)). Fig. S2(iii) shows that the fluorescence spectra of freshly prepared and 3-month-old aqueous dispersion of N/B-CNPs. It is apparent that the fluorescence intensity of N/B-CNPs remained unchanged even after 3 months of storage. Thus, it can be inferred that the N/B-CNPs are quite stable and can retain their fluorescence properties for an extended period of time. Further, the calculated QY of N/B-CNPs was found to be 29.70 % which is higher than that of the microwave synthesised carbon based QDs [22]. The changes in fluorescence intensity of N/B-CNPs under different pH conditions are shown in Fig. S2(iv). As demonstrated in derived from o-phenylenediamine Fig. S2(iv), the emission intensities of N/B-CNPs steadily increased upto pH 7, and then gradually decreased (pH 10), demonstrating the pH dependency. The surface charge analysis of N/B-CNPs solution was measured and it is displayed in Fig. S3. It is apparent that the Zeta potential of N/B-CNPs was found to be −35.6 mV demonstrating that the N/B-CNPs surface was enriched with negatively charged functional groups. It is well known that the solutions with Zeta potential values higher than + 20 mV and lower than −20 mV are considered to be more stable [27]. The changes in the photophysical behavior of N/B-CNPs in acidic and basic conditions can be best explained on the basis of the surface charge. At acidic condition, the negatively charged N/B-CNPs gets protonated, whereas, in the basic condition surface functional group could undergo deprotonation thereby resulting in an increase in the emission intensity. It has been reported earlier that the surface charge and fluorescence intensity of N/B-CNPs are prone to undergo fluctuation in the protonation and deprotonation process at different pH levels [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and functional groups analysis of N/B-CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: To find out the surface morphology, particle size of N/B-CNPs and HR-TEM analysis were recorded and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 2 . As seen in Fig. 2(i), the prepared N/B-CNPs are spherically shaped with no apparent agglomeration [22]. Further, the average particle size of the N/B-CNPs was found to be 13 ± 3.94 nm is given in Fig. 2(ii) [28]. As shown in Fig. 2(iii), HR-TEM micrograph shows well resolved lattice fringes with a d - spacing of about 0.19 nm which almost corresponds to the (101) in plane spacing of the graphitic sp2 carbon. It has been documented that CNPs are known to show amorphous or lattice structure under HR-TEM. In the present case, contrary to the observation from the Raman analysis (vide infra), HR-TEM micrograph of CNPs showed distinctive fringes which could be either attributed to the formation of ordered polymer core structure or graphitic structure in CNPs. Moreover, the slight deviation in lattice space value of CNPs from that of the graphite lattice spaces could be attributed to the variation evoked by a possible polymer/carbon hybrid structure formation. Furthermore, the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy analysis of N/B-CNPs is shown in Fig. S4. As presented in Fig. S4, the elemental composition of N/B-CNPs is studied by EDX analysis and it was revealed that the N/B-CNPs are composed of mainly carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and boron.

The FT-IR and Raman analysis were performed to get insights on the functional group compositions of N/B-CNPs. Fig. 3 (i) illustrates the FT-IR spectrum of synthesized N/B-CNPs, the peaks located at 3434, 3318 and 3212 cm−1 are ascribed to the presence of B-OH, B-NH and aromatic C-H, stretching vibrations, respectively [22,26,29]. The presence of hydroxyl, boron and amino groups integrated on the edge surface of N/B-CNPs, increased its solubility in water. The peak located at 1652 cm−1 and 1508 cm−1 affirmed the presence of C = C, N-H / B-N, respectively [30,31]. The presence of B-O-H stretching vibration located at 1346 cm1 and 1226 cm−1 can be attributed to the = C-H bending vibration. The peak at 1114 cm−1 corresponds to the B-O-H bending vibration [32]. In addition, the two absorption peaks at 856 cm−1 and 761 cm−1 could be ascribed to the = C-H out of plane bending vibrations [33]. Fig. 3(ii) shows the Ramanspectrum of N/B-CNPs and it depicts two main peaks at 1348 cm−1 and 1618 cm−1 corresponding to D and G bands, respectively. The graphitic peak (G band) is attributed to the E2g C-C bond vibrational stretching and the disorder peak (D band) around 1330–1350 cm−1 corresponds to the A1g vibrational mode. The calculated relative Raman intensity ratio of ID/IG about 1.20 clearly depicts the formation of defective graphitized structure [34,35].

To analyze the formation mechanism of N/B-CNPs from o-phenylenediamine and boric acid, 1H, 13C and 11B NMR spectral characterizations were carried out and are displayed in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4(i & ii), 1H NMR spectra of N/B-CNPs clearly demonstrated the presence of sp2 carbon, while, 13C NMR spectrum showed signals in the range of 39.26 to 40.51 ppm corresponding to sp3 carbon. The signals between 102.29 and 144.80 ppm represents the presence of sp2 carbon. The obtained NMR signals for N/B-CNPs are assigned as follows: 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ 7.89–7.92 (1H, q, J = 3.4 Hz, Ar-H), 7.55–7.57 (1H, q, J = 3 4 Hz, Ar-H), 6.92 (2H, s, N-H), 6.53–6.55 (1H, dd, J = 3.44 Hz Ar-H), 6.40–6.43 (1H, dd, J = 3.48 Hz, Ar-H), 3.55 (1H, brs –OH). All these results indicate the possible formation of 2,3-diaminophenazine. However, the observance of signal around δ 3.55 ppm indicates the presence of alcoholic functional group which could be due to the formation of 2-amino-3-hydroxyphenazine as well. The origin and formation mechanism of N/B-CNPs derived from o-phenylenediamine has already been reported [36]. The formation of 2,3-diaminophenazine and 2-amino-3-hydroxyphenazine fluorophores during the oxidation process of o-phenylenediamine under hydrothermal conditions are shown to contribute the fluorescence of the N/B-CNPs derived from o-phenylenediamine [37]. However, it has been reported that 2,3-diaminophenazine was found to be the dominant fluorophore and tend to contribute majorly to the yellow emission of N/B-CNPs rather than 2-amino-3-hydroxyphenazine [38]. To ascertain the role of boric acid in the present work, 11B NMR analysis of N/B-CNPs was carried out and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. S5. It is apparent that signal at 19.48 ppm clearly suggested that the presence of boron functional group (Fig. S5). Thus, from the 1H, 13C and 11B NMR studies, we infer that at the initial phase of the reaction o-phenylenediamine gets oxidized to form 2,3-diaminophenazine which then undergoes polymerization and cyclisation reaction to from the core structure [36–38]. The initial carbonization of 2,3-diaminophenazine ultimately leads to the aromatization of carbon core, which is then covered with abundant boron, hydroxyl and amine functional group on its edge surfaces (Scheme 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N/B-CNPs As fluorescent probe for glyphosate (turn-off)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorometric response of N/B-CNPs probe towards various metal ions and other pesticide molecules were investigated and it was found that considerable changes in the emission intensity was noticed upon the addition of glyphosate. Emission titration experiments were carried out to study the fluorometric response of N/B-CNPs in the presence of varying concentrations of glyphosate (Fig. 5). It is obvious from Fig. 5(i) that in the absence of glyphosate N/B-CNPs exhibited an emission maximum at 566 nm, upon excitation at 400 nm. The addition of increasing concentrations of glyphosate to the aqueous dispersion of N/B-CNPs resulted in a gradual decrease in the emission intensities of the signal probe without any remarkable peak shift/change in the emission maxima.

Further, the extent of fluorescence quenching was found to be directly proportional to the concentration of the added glyphosate. The emission quenching data obtained from the steady state fluorescence analysis was then utilized to construct the calibration plot between the relative emission intensity versus the concentrations of glyphosate. As shown in Fig. 5(ii), the calibration plot showed a good linear relation over the concentrations of glyphosate range from 5.00 to 85.00 µM with a good correlation coefficient. The calculated limit of detection value was found to be 0.088 µM, which is much lower than the permitted levels of glyphosate contamination of drinking water prescribed by the USA [39]. Moreover, the obtained LOD value from the present work was better or comparable to that of the previously reported detection assays for glyphosate (Table S1).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism in organic fluorophores or fluorescent materials are generally caused by four processes including Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), ground state complex formation, inner filter effect (IFE) and charge transfer or electron transfer. To get further insights into the glyphosate induced fluorescence quenching mechanism in N/B-CNPs, the fluorescence quenching data was analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation - (3) and the plot is given in Fig. S6 [40].
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
k
sv
Q
where, F0 is the fluorescence emission intensity of N/B-CNPs and F is the fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of glyphosate,
k
sv
is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] represents the concentration of glyphosate and τ0 is the average lifetime of the N/B-CNPs (1.62 ns).

As shown in Fig. S6, the Stern-Volmer plot showed a good linear relationship for the plot between F0 / F vs concentrations of glyphosate ranging from 5.00 to 85.00 µM, with excellent correlation coefficient value of R2 = 0.99. In general, a linear Stern-Volmer plot is indicative of a single class of quenching process i.e., it indicates the occurrence either a static or dynamic quenching mechanism. Thus, in the present case, the quenching of N/B-CNPs could be either caused by a static or dynamic quenching mechanism. The quenching constant calculated for the emission quenching data of N/B-CNPs in the presence of glyphosate was found to be ksv= 8660 mol-1 dm-3, whereas, the
k q
calculated using the relationship
k q
=
k
sv
/τ0 yielded a value of 5.34 × 1012 dm3 mol−1 s−1. Based on the previous report, the diffusion-controlled quenching is indicated by a value of 1010 dm3 mol−1 s−1 [40]. Interestingly, in the present study
k q
value is larger than the typical diffusion controlled quenching process showing that the N/B-CNPs quenching is mainly driven by static or radiative quenching mechanism.

The time resolved fluorescence lifetime analysis is an ideal tool to distinguish between static and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) quenching mechanism. The average fluorescence lifetime study of N/B-CNPs before and after addition of glyphosate/ClO- is shown in Fig. 6 . The excited state decay profile of N/B-CNPs is fitted to a monoexponentialy curve with a life time of about 1.62 ns. After the addition of glyphosate, the lifetime remained nearly constant at 1.60 ns. It is apparent that the addition of glyphosate did not elicit any changes in the fluorescence lifetime of N/B-CNPs. This clearly suggest that the formation of ground-state complexes between N/B-CNPs and glyphosate.

The selectivity of N/B-CNPs probe was analyzed by carrying out the glyphosate detection assay in the presence of other interferences including Ag+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pd2+,K+, Na+, Cu2+, Mg2+, CN–, I-, S2-,Cl-, ClO-, histidine, methionine, threonine, dopamine, fenthion, lysine, phenylalanine, proline, daunorubicin and parathion-methyl. The N/B-CNPs detection probe was mixed with a known concentrations of glyphosate and interfering molecules/ions. The solution mixture was then incubated and subsequently emission spectral analysis were carried out which are shown in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7, even in the presence of other interferences the changes in the relative emission intensity of N/B-CNPs remained almost same as compared to that of glyphosate suggesting the excellent selectivity of probe.

The practical applicability of the proposed fluorometric N/B-CNPs probe for glyphosate detection was evaluated for its performance in real ground water samples are given in Table 1 . Table 1 shows that the N/B-CNPs probe showed excellent sensitivity and appreciable recoveries, indicating its potential in detecting glyphosate in ground water sample.

The fluorometric response of the N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system toward different anions was performed and it was found that the addition of hypochlorite (ClO-) evoked a significant fluorescence recovery. Emission spectra of N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system under different concentrations of ClO- are studied and the resultant spectra is shown in Fig. 8 (i). As seen in Fig. 8(i), with increasing ClO- concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N/B-CNPs recovered gradually (turn-on). The enhancement in fluorescence intensities at 566 nm is not accompanied with any shift in the wavelength maxima and the fluorescence recovery was found to be proportional to that of the concentration of ClO-. The recovery in fluorescence intensities could be attributed to the ClO- induced dissociation of N/B-CNPs/glyphosate complex. It has been reported earlier that in a highly chlorinated environment, glyphosate molecules tend to get degraded rapidly [41]. Zeta potential analysis of N/B-CNPs in the absence and presence of glyphosate and ClO- was carried out to get further insights into the possible dissociation of N/B-CNPs/glyphosate complex upon the addition of ClO- (Fig. S7). As seen in Fig. S7, after the addition of glyphosate pristine N/B-CNPs showed a considerable increase in its Zeta potential value from −36.1 mV to −27.3 mV. However, in the presence of ClO-, the Zeta potential of the N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system exhibited a significant decrease to −36.1 mV, which is almost comparable to that of the pristine N/B-CNPs. This clearly, indicates that the addition of ClO- evoked the dissociation of N/B-CNPs/glyphosate complexes. Further, the UV-visible absorption spectral studies of N/B-CNPs in the absence and presence of glyphosate is shown in Fig. S8. It can be seen from Fig. S8, that upon the addition of glyphosate the absorption intensity of N/B-CNPs showed a marginal decrease, which is indicative of the ground state-complex formation. Interestingly, in the presence of hypochlorite the absorption intensity of N/B-CNPs showed a recovery, suggesting the destabilization of N/B-CNPs-glyphosate complex (Fig. S8). This, could be attributed to the fact that sodium hypochlorite is known to oxidize glyphosate into chloramine compounds, thereby resulting in the dissociation of the N/B-CNPs-glyphosate complex and subsequent fluorescence enhancement [42,43]. Fig. 8(ii), shows that the calibration plot between the relative emission intensities and different concentrations of ClO- exhibits good linearity over the range from 5.00 to 50.00 µM. Further, the computed LOD is found to be 0.12 nM (Table S2), which is much lower than the permissible (58.3 µM) levels of chlorine concentration prescribed by the WHO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system probe (turn-on)
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of N/B-CNPs/glyphosate system towards hypochlorite was evaluated by performing the interference analysis in the presence of various common interferences including ClO-, I-, Cl-, CN–, CH3COO–, Br-, S2 -, H2O2, NO2 – and SO4 2- and the corresponding results are depicted in Fig. 9 . As illustrated in Fig. 9, the addition of common interferants did not influence the ClO- induced fluorescence recovery of N/B-CNPs-glyphosate system. This clearly indicates the excellent selectivity of the proposed turn-on detection assay. The possibility of N/B-CNPs-glyphosate system as a sensitive fluorometric probe towards hypochlorite was evaluated by performing the assay in real water samples (Table 2 ). As listed in Table 2, the N/B-CNPs-glyphosate turn-on probe showed remarkable selectivity towards ClO- with recoveries ranging between 90.5 and 97%, demonstrating the practical applicability of the proposed assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent N/B-CNPs paper strip and polymer thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared bright yellow fluorescence N/B-CNPs was utilized to fabricate a fluorescent polymer thin-film. To accomplish this, 1 g of PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) has been dissolved by stirring along with 30 mL of double distilled water at room temperature for 8 h. After complete dissolution of PVA, 0.5 µ/L of N/B-CNPs were added to the solution [44]. After blending for 12 h, the uniformly blended solution was poured into a polypropylene petri dish and placed in a hot air oven at 50 °C. The N/B-CNPs embedded fluorescent polymer thin-film under visible and UV light is shown in Fig. 10 . It can be seen from Fig. 10(i) that in the visible light obtained from N/B-CNPs films are highly flexible and transparent to naked eye. However, under UV irradiation, the polymer thin-film showed bright yellow fluorescence. This suggests that the N/B-CNPs are embedded within the polymer matrix and retained its emission property in solid matrix as well. The developed fluorescent polymer thin film has the potential to be applied in different applications such as light-emitting diodes and smart packing materials. Furthermore, the N/B-CNPs have been utilized to develop a paper-based strip for the turn-off–on detection assay. The test strips were made by dipping the uniformly cut Whatman filter papers into the N/B-CNPs solution and then the completely wet papers were then subjected to drying under mild heating conditions. The completely dried papers were then used for simultaneous detection of glyphosate and ClO- (Fig. 10(ii)). It is apparent that test strips showed turn-off and turn-on response under UV light illumination for glyphosate and ClO-, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo toxicity and bio-imaging of N/B-CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Plants are a fundamental base component of ecosystems and the use of carbon-based nanomaterials in environmental applications have direct implication on the growth of flora and fauna [45]. As a result, it is important to study the impact of nanomaterial exposure on plants. Factors such as the intake, translocation and accumulation of N/B-CNPs in plants will have a significant impact on their growth. The excellent fluorescence property of N/B-CNPs, on the other hand, can offer consistent and unique optical properties that make them ideal for high-resolution and bio-imaging of plants. In the present work, mung bean seeds of comparable size were cultivated in clean water and exposed to different dosages of N/B-CNPs (0.1, 0.4, 0.7, 1.0 and 1.5 mg/mL), when mung bean seeds were germinated with various concentrations of N/B-CNPs, it was observed that the seeds germinated with radicles in both the N/B-CNPs solution and pure water (control). The development of mung bean sprouts varied significantly between the control and N/B-CNPs -treated groups. The concentration range of N/B-CNPs from 0 to 1.5 mg/mL was observed to promote mung bean development. The concentration-dependent promoting effect is clearly demonstrated by a digital photograph of mung bean sprouts in daylight (Fig. 11 (i)). The root and stem elongation were also explored to further investigate the dose response on the phytotoxicity of N/B-CNPs during the mung bean growing period are given in Fig. 11(ii & iii). The root and stem extended to variable degrees in different concentrations of N/B-CNPs when compared to that of the control group. Up until the application with different concentrations of N/B-CNPs, which significantly boosted (p 0.01) root and stem elongation, the lengths of the root and stem showed a growing tendency. As seen in Fig. 11(iv & v) the uptake of N/B-CNPs by mung bean can be identified in vivo due to the bright yellow fluorescence of N/B-CNPs compared with the control group under bright field images of root. These results suggested that N/B-CNPs did not show any adverse phytotoxicity in mung bean and its fluorescence property can be utilized for effective biological imaging.

In summary, fluorescent N/B-CNPs were prepared using one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesized N/B-CNPs exhibited a good photostability, different ionic strength, bright yellow fluorescence upto three months stability and showed excitation independent emission properties. The obtained fluorescent N/B-CNPs was utilized as a fluorometric probe for simultaneous detection of glyphosate (turn-off) and hypochlorite (turn-on) with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. Further, turn-off–on detection assay was utilized to detect glyphosate (turn-off) and hypochlorite (turn-on) in real water samples, respectively, with appreciable recoveries. Furthermore, dose dependent phytotoxicity assay showed that N/B-CNPs did not elicit adverse physiological changes in the plant growth, indicating its biocompatible nature. The fluorescence imaging of N/B-CNPs treated mung bean (Vigna Radiata) roots revealed the accumulation and distribution of fluorescent N/B-CNPs within the plant roots. Moreover, the fabricated N/B-CNPs embedded paper-based detection probes and fluorescent thin film can be explored in detection assays and smart packaging materials.

Karuppasamy Nandhini: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Malaichamy Ilanchelian: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: K. Nandhini acknowledges the project RUSA-2.0, BCTRC Allotment number-CT26A, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India for financial support. Prof. M. Ilanchelian acknowledges the DST-SERB-EEQ project no. EEQ/2021/000469, dt: 02.03.2022 India for the financial support. We gratefully thank and dedicate it to our beloved Dr. S. Abraham John, Professor, Gandhigram Rural Institute, Dindigul, India for his kind support, encouragement and valuable guidance. Authors acknowledges the NMR facility, CIC, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, supported by DST-PURSE Phase-II program. Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology Madras is acknowledged for providing lifetime analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114869.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
